entry.title: HTTPS Sender adapter in iFlow shows sometimes a 401 error
entry.content:
Hi

I have an iFlow with a HTTPS sender adapter. We created a specific role and assigned the role to this iFlow and a clientID (generated in BTP). In general everything is working fine. An external partner is calling the endpoint and sending messages (XML IDOCs).

However we now have a situation that a specific message (relatively large, size 1MB, XML IDOC) is not being processed by the HTTPS sender adapter.

When checking the Integration Suite Logs I see the following that a HTTP 401 is returned.

Also a successfull request is having 401s but will get a 200 in a next request and will continue.

How do I need to interpret this and how can this be solved?

Thanks

Ron

entry.title: WFA on HANA user not being captured
entry.content:
HI @YSE-1 ,

While validating the configuration, we have found 1 user not showing up in any of the counts.

WFA is set to capture data only starting 01.01.2019

WFA is set to capture hire/rehire/transfer/promotion/demotion/termination.

This user has been hired in ec in 2018. On that same day he had other 4 events out of which one is a transfer and the other 3 are data changes.

I am not sure in this scenario if he should still be picked up by the hire event since we do not capture as per configuration mentioned above (2018 hire <2019 wfa data). Maybe it could be impacted by the transfer that happened the same day as the hire?

Other than this I do not see any reason why the user would not show up - he is active and never had a termination/rehire - only many data changes.

Kind regards,

Alex

entry.title: Error 407 When Connecting SAP CAP to ECC via On-Premise Destination Only in Production Subaccount
entry.content:
I am facing a 407 Proxy Authentication Required error when connecting my SAP CAP application to ECC via an On-Premise Destination in SAP BTP. Strangely, this issue occurs only in the production subaccount, while the connection works fine in dev, QAS, and pre-production subaccounts.

Has anyone encountered this issue before? Any suggestions on how to troubleshoot or resolve it?

entry.title: Request to add Restriction Type in SD Roles
entry.content:
This it to request to add Sales Group/Customer Group as part of restriction types as there is no more time to redesign the SD process based on client requirements. Sales Group represents the sales department and each sales department has set of encoders and sales managers. Thus, this field is needed as well for workflow approval restriction for Sales Managers in Quotations and Sales Orders.

 

The restriction type Sales Group should be added in the following roles:
BR_INTERNAL_SALES_REP
BR_INTERNAL_SALES_REP_MNGR
BR_SALES_MANAGER

entry.title: Planning your System Conversion to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition following SAP Activate
entry.content:
We just launched an independent Roadmap Viewer for SAP Activate for RISE with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition - system Conversion. In this blog post, I want to give you an overview what you can find there.

Let’s begin with the basics: What is SAP Activate?

SAP Activate is the methodology used to implement SAP solutions. It follows an Agile approach and simplifies project management by breaking it down into manageable tasks across various workstreams, such as Project Management, Solution Adoption, and Customer Team Enablement. While many of these tasks overlap no matter how you approach the move to SAP S/4HANA, others are specific to the transition path selected.

This blog post series intends to provide a comprehensive overview of the specific activities to address at each stage of a system conversion project. By effectively mastering these tasks, the project team will be better prepared to navigate the complexities of a conversion project, thereby facilitating a smooth transition to the new system.

This graph gives a quick overview of the task I am talking about:

Let’s take a closer look at the planning phases. It means Discover and Prepare.

Discover Your Solution

The Discover phase is about what happens before you officially kick off your SAP S/4HANA project. By the time you finish this phase, your organization should have a clear digital transformation strategy and a solid roadmap or plan for moving to SAP S/4HANA, supported by a business case. During this phase, you'll also pick your partner and decide on the transition approach—whether that’s a new implementation, system conversion or selective data transition.

You’ll start with strategic planning, where you’ll outline your innovation strategy and high-level roadmap. This step also involves making some initial decisions about security and analytics. Next up is the fun part—trial system and application value and scoping, where you’ll assess how much value SAP S/4HANA can bring to your organization and check your readiness for taking the leap.

Here are some key tasks to focus on during this phase:

Let’s check them out a bit more closely:

Experience SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

To really understand the potential benefits of SAP S/4HANA during the Discover phase, it helps to have access to an actual SAP S/4HANA system. You can set up a 30-day trial system pretty quickly using the SAP Cloud Appliance Library. It’s hosted on Microsoft Azure, AWS, or Google Cloud, but keep in mind that hosting costs apply, and you’ll need an account with the cloud provider.

If you've already signed a deal with RISE with SAP, you might qualify for a Starter System for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. This 90-day trial comes with the fully activated appliance template, managed by the SAP Enterprise Cloud Services team. It’s a great way to explore the system and run fit-to-standard workshops.

Identify the Value of SAP S/4HANA on Existing Business Processes

As you dive into strategic planning, your business process experts should create a more detailed solution mapping at the business scenario level. To pinpoint issues in your processes, you can request the SAP Signavio Process Insights report. This report also provides personalized recommendations for improving the processes and highlights the right innovations for your transformation journey.

Conversion Readiness Assessment

As you're moving from an existing SAP ERP solution, you'll want to evaluate what it will take to run a system conversion to SAP S/4HANA. The SAP Readiness Check for SAP S/4HANA is a super useful, automated tool that helps you assess your current SAP ERP before your transformation project kicks off. It identifies simplification items that could affect your system, as well as analyzing custom code, add-ons, business functions, and suggested SAP Fiori apps. Some issues might be significant roadblocks you need to address before you get started.

This tool also tells you about compatibility features still in use, which are functionalities created for SAP ERP. Just a heads up: usage rights for these features will expire soon. So, it’s essential to replace these features as part of your system conversion.

The results of this analysis will give you a list of pre-projects you need to handle in the next phase.

Prepare your System Conversion

The primary focus during this phase is organizing all the planning details. This is when your project officially begins. This includes defining your objectives and creating a detailed plan. Additionally, you assign members to the project team.

One of the big technical points to nail down is the system conversion strategy. You’ll be figuring out the best way to convert the system, setting up conversion cycles, and planning for any dual maintenance that might be needed. It’s also the time to wrap up any tech prep tasks and set up fit-to-standard workshops for some specific business processes.

Here are some key tasks to focus on during this phase:

Let’s check them more thoroughly.

Technical Project Preparation

Technical project prep means handling key tasks in the existing SAP ERP before diving into the main SAP S/4HANA project. Here’s what you need to focus on:

  • Check Interoperability: Before you start, you need to make sure that all SAP systems talking to the future SAP S/4HANA system are on compatible versions. This will help you spot any issues that could pop up during the transition. If so, you should add any extra tasks needed to the project plan.
  • Go for a Clean Core Approach: This is the time to find chances to shift any custom code to more standard options. It helps keep things stable, cuts down on maintenance costs, and opens the door for more innovation. Using the Cloud Readiness Check from the ABAP Test Cockpit can help you see how the custom code stacks up against the ABAP for Cloud Development standards. You’ll also need to figure out when to make any code changes, either in a later phase or together with custom code fixes.
  • Define Data Validation Tools: For the S/4HANA conversion, it’s key to ensure that the business data is migrating accurately. You’ll need to pick the right tools to keep everything consistent and check the data’s integrity before and after the conversion.
  • Know the Technical Team's Role: In an SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition project, the tech team has slightly different responsibilities compared to those working on an on-premise SAP ERP. The SAP Enterprise Cloud Services team usually handles infrastructure and maintenance. However, your tech experts and your partner are still important. They will manage the system configurations, performance, user roles, and integration with other systems. They’ll also take care of SAP Fiori and SAP BTP administration and act as contacts with SAP for any technical issues or service requests.

Getting Pre-Conversion Projects Done

Before you kick off the first conversion cycle, it’s crucial to wrap up all pre-conversion projects identified in the Conversion Readiness Assessment. Some examples of these projects include:

  • Managing data volumes
  • Syncing customers/vendors into business partners (CVI)
  • Reconciling financial data
  • Checking add-on compatibility
  • Cleaning up unused custom code
  • Unicode conversion
  • Running a full sandbox conversion

Transition Planning

This activity is all about figuring out the scope and execution plan for the upcoming SAP S/4HANA transition project. The end result will be an initial action plan, which will lay the groundwork for a more detailed project plan. You’ll keep fine-tuning this plan throughout the project, especially during the Prepare and Explore phases, as part of the project management efforts. The main tasks here are

  • Defining Scope and Objectives: You’ll put together a scope document that clearly outlines the IT and business goals. This will include:
    • Success Criteria: What does success look like for this project?
    • Starting Point: Which SAP solutions are you keeping, replacing, or updating?
    • Target Solution: What new features and releases are you aiming for, plus any quick wins?
  • Cutover Strategy: You’ll document your strategy to move to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. Since the cutover activities need to happen on a tight schedule, it's super important to plan the cutover strategy early and get everything ready. That way, you can avoid any delays down the road.
  • Custom Code Analysis: As you convert to SAP S/4HANA, it’s crucial to analyze the existing custom code. Here’s the plan:
    • Get familiar with how you’ll manage code changes.
    • Understand the SAP clean core strategy.
    • Use SAP tools to flag any unused code for cleanup.
    • Identify which custom ABAP code needs adjustments.
    • Figure out what can be moved to one of the new extensibility options or replaced with standard SAP features.
    • Estimate the work involved, aiming for quick fixes wherever you can.
  • Technical Architecture and Security Update: you’ll need to revise the current technical architecture to align with the scope document, possibly switching to an SAP HANA database and incorporating SAP Fiori components. It’s also important to document new security requirements, especially around roles and SAP Fiori. A self-check on technical security will be part of this.
  • Data Volume Planning: Before your transition, you need to manage the data volume to minimize conversion time and hardware costs. This means identifying which data can be cleaned up before you start the real work.
  • Assessing Interfaces and Integration: you’ll look at the existing documentation to pinpoint external systems and business objects that need to integrate with SAP S/4HANA. It’s also important to assess how moving to SAP S/4HANA will affect the current interfaces. Standard interfaces should remain stable, but you need to watch out for any custom interfaces that might be impacted.

System Conversion Planning

Let’s break down what you need to do for the system conversion. This phase is all about figuring out the technical approach to the conversion, including how many times you’ll need to convert and setting up a dual maintenance strategy. Here’s what you need to focus on:

  • Choosing the Right Conversion Approach: SAP has different methods for system conversion and ways to minimize downtime, depending on what you need (like an in-place conversion or moving things to a new infrastructure). It's super important to look at these options early on and pick what works best for your project. This decision can really affect the project plan, timelines, and costs, so make sure you evaluate everything.
  • Planning the Conversion Cycles: To make sure everything goes smoothly when transitioning to SAP S/4HANA, you need to set up a solid conversion process. This means deciding how many conversion cycles you’ll have and in what order. A good rule of thumb is to plan for at least five cycles: a sandbox, development, quality assurance, a dry run, and the final production. If you run into any issues or choose a specific conversion method, you might need to do more cycles.
  • Considering Dual Maintenance: During the conversion, there might be times when the business needs to make changes to the production environment before everything is finalized. This means you may need a temporary setup to handle these changes while the main system is converting to SAP S/4HANA. Also, any changes made will need to sync up with the newly converted systems. So, it’s important to figure out if you need a dual maintenance process.

User Experience Planning and Workshops

The UX planning and workshops are here to help you manage and boost your UX adoption over time. Since UX covers a lot of different areas, the goal is to get business and tech teams working together more effectively. You can take advantage of the recommendations you'll get during and after your move to SAP S/4HANA whenever you're ready to ramp up your UX adoption and add some real value to your business.

This includes information on SAP S/4HANA and the related RISE with SAP extensions on the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). You’ll find a User Experience Strategy Workshop and a handy template for nailing down guiding principles and governance. There are also workshop templates to help you establish business value, outline future goals, and brainstorm high-value apps and features as you update your UX adoption roadmap. Plus, you'll get tips on how to select apps, check if they fit your needs, explore extension options, design launchpad layouts, and communicate changes to users.

Fit-to-Standard Prep for Specific Features

First things first: you’ll want to get to know the fit-to-standard framework and prep for the fit-to-standard workshops. When you’re doing a system conversion, these workshops are a direct result of the Conversion Readiness Assessment, specifically the Conduct Functional Review Workshop task during the discovery phase. The simplification items you’ll review in these workshops generally fall into three groups:

  • Group 1: Needs functional/technical fixes 
  • Group 2: Needs fit-to-standard workshops 
  • Group 3: Not applicable

In this task, you will focus on Group 2, meaning those items that need fit-to-standard workshops. Here are a couple of examples:

  • Features in SAP ERP that aren’t in SAP S/4HANA: For instance, you’d implement Settlement Management instead of using SD rebates processing.
  • Features available in SAP S/4HANA; but are in compatibility scope: For instance, you’ll want to switch to Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) instead of Warehouse Management (WM).

Don’t forget to include functions you’re not currently using that you’ll implement during your system conversion project.

The fit-to-standard analysis and design includes the following activities:

During Prepare, you must focus on the first two:

  • Workshop Preparation: These workshops are divided up by Lines of Business (like finance) and end-to-end solutions (like accounting and financial close). When doing a system conversion, the focus is on where the solution changes. You’ll start with an initial fit-to-standard workshop and, if needed, follow it up with a design workshop. The project manager will handle the workshop schedule and make sure everyone knows the boundaries and integration points. You'll have a standard set of documents to guide both input and output.
  • Select the right system: It's super important to choose the right system for these activities. You’ll want to consider several options, like the Starter System for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, the SAP S/4HANA Fully-Activated Appliance, or a previously converted sandbox system (if you have one). Don’t forget to wrap up any pending tasks, like enabling SAP Fiori, so everything is set for the workshops.

Once you've wrapped up those activities, you’re all set to start your system conversion. To get ready, check out the second blog post in this series: Running your System Conversion to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition following SAP Activate.

We've come to the end of this blog post.  Just a reminder, this was a quick overview of the tasks you can find in the SAP Activate Roadmap Viewer for RISE with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition—System Conversion. Check out the details in the Roadmap Viewer to help you plan your system conversion project.

I hope you found this blog post helpful. If you did, please give it a "like" or share it. I’d love to hear your thoughts and feedback, so feel free to leave a comment. If you have any questions, just drop them in the Q&A section with the tags SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. We'll try to get back to you as soon as we can.


Thanks,

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA RIG team.

entry.title: Running your System Conversion to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition following SAP Activate
entry.content:
As explained in part one of this blog series, Planning your System Conversion to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition following SAP Activate, SAP Activate follows an Agile approach and simplifies project management by breaking it down into manageable tasks across various workstreams, such as Project Management, Solution Adoption, and Customer Team Enablement. While many of these tasks overlap no matter how you approach the move to SAP S/4HANA, others are specific to the transition path selected.

This blog post series intends to provide a comprehensive overview of the specific activities to address at each stage of a system conversion project. By effectively mastering these tasks, the project team will be better prepared to navigate the complexities of a conversion project, thereby facilitating a smooth transition to the new system.

This graph gives a quick overview of the task I am talking about:

Let’s take a closer look from Explore to Run.

Explore the Solution

The Explore phase involves running the first conversion of a sandbox system. Most of the configurations can stay the same, but there are a few things to keep in mind after the conversion, like getting SAP Fiori up and running and fixing any inconsistencies in the live system before you move on to the next round of conversions.

Once the conversion is complete, the project team will perform initial testing to ensure that everything that shouldn’t have been affected remains fine. You’ll also use this environment to spot opportunities to improve processes with SAP Fiori.

Since this first conversion might bring up some unexpected issues, you’ll need to take a close look at your project plan and make adjustments as needed. If you find any big problem, you might have to do a few more iterations in the sandbox.

Additionally, you'll dive deep into the current custom code and review the integrations. This analysis isn’t just about listing what’ll be affected; it’s also about figuring out how and when to switch over to options that fit with clean core principles.

Here are some key tasks to focus on during this phase:

Let’s check them out a bit more closely:

System Conversion Execution of the Sandbox System

In system conversion, you start by creating an SAP S/4HANA sandbox system from the existing SAP ERP production system and perform a full conversion. The goal is to have a working sandbox and check if the process works for the business, along with an initial downtime estimate. You can't move on to the Realize phase until the sandbox conversion is successful.

Here’s a quick rundown of the steps involved:

  1. Technical Prep: After cloning the production system, make sure the sandbox meets all technical requirements. Check compatibility with add-ons and tools, and update any necessary SAP Notes. Document everything for future reference, which means creating a Runbook.
  2. Functional Prep: Before diving into the technical conversion, tackle any SAP S/4HANA simplification items that need fixing in the existing SAP ERP. Resolve errors from the simplification Item check. Also, run and log results from the business data validation reports.
  3. System Conversion: Use the SUM tool to technically convert to SAP S/4HANA. Once you’ve completed the conversion and some follow-up tasks, provide the necessary info to the SAP Enterprise Cloud Services team for their post-processing.
  4. Post-Conversion: For mandatory post-processing, especially in finance, follow the “Conversion Guide for SAP S/4HANA.” Run the business data validation reports again and compare results with the source system. If there are discrepancies, investigate and ensure it’s okay to proceed.

Additional Activities in the Converted Sandbox System

After you finish the technical conversion, there are a few steps to make sure everything's ready for the next phase:

  1. Enable SAP Fiori: You’ll need to activate SAP Fiori in your converted sandbox. This involves setting up necessary frameworks and running the SAP Fiori activation procedure to enable the different SAP Fiori apps and their dependencies. It's a great time to check out new features and set up your initial SAP security roles.
  2. Run Initial Testing: Validate that the conversion didn’t mess with any unexpected processes by running your usual end-to-end scenarios. Consider using SAP Fiori for some steps to see its value and rethink your user experience strategy.
  3. Fix Inconsistencies: Address any issues you found before and after the conversion in your productive landscape. For master data, fix things directly in the productive system. For broader issues, handle them in the development system and transport the fixes accordingly. Completing this now will make future conversions easier.

Fit-to-Standard Workshops for Specific Features

The fit-to-standard analysis and design process helps get approval for changes to the project scope and design before moving into the realization phase. These workshops come from the Conversion Readiness Assessment done in the Discover phase.

During the Functional Review Workshop, you identify key areas for simplification that need to be tackled in the fit-to-standard workshops. This involves SAP ERP features that either aren’t in SAP S/4HANA or are in its Compatibility scope, which will be replaced during the conversion. It can also include features that aren’t currently used but will be added as part of the project.

Just to refresh your mind, the fit-to-standard analysis and design includes the following tasks:

Here are the activities to be conducted during the Explore phase:

  • Fit-to-Standard Workshops & Documentation: These workshops help ensure the solution meets the business needs and processes set in the Prepare phase. Business process experts explore functionalities and figure out any additional requirements, which get documented in the project backlog. It's crucial to document all decisions and requirements from the workshops. you’ll analyze and make sure nothing is missing. Technical experts also document all the change requirements in detail.
  • Validating Requirements: Solution architects and project managers work together to ensure all requirements are accurately captured and classified. This helps prioritize which requirements to focus on and decide whether to keep old features or adopt new ones.
  • Design Workshops & documentation: Next, you run a design workshop to document configurations and custom development needed based on the delta requirements prioritized earlier. This is crucial for accurate estimates in the realization phase.
  • Final Review and Sign-Off: The project architects review the design and make necessary adjustments, ensuring all parties agree on the requirements and proposed solutions. They document and categorize any issues and update the backlog for the realization phase. Once they have the official approval and necessary documents, the project confidently moves to the next phase.

Custom Code Deep-Dive Analysis

 When you're gearing up for a system conversion to SAP S/4HANA, an important part of the process is figuring out what to do with your custom code. You’ll need to sort it into three categories: “must-change,” “should-change,” or “nice-to-change.” Ideally, you should kick off this analysis during the Prepare phase, in the “Transition Planning” activity.

To dive into your custom code, you can use a few handy SAP tools, including:

  • Usage Monitor (Transaction SUSG): This tool helps you see how often your custom code is actually being used.
  • Custom Code Migration App: It helps spot and package up code that isn’t used anymore.
  • Software Update Manager (SUM): This is great for automatically cleaning up unused code during the conversion.
  • ABAP Test Cockpit (Transaction ATC): This tool checks your custom code for any simplifications needed for SAP S/4HANA and suggests ways to fix them automatically.
  • Transactions SPDD and SPAU: These are used to tweak dictionary objects and custom code.

Once you get results from these tools, you should put together a prioritized list of custom code items. Focus on what needs to change. With this list in hand, you'll make some key design decisions. You’ll have to decide whether to update the code to fit new SAP features, completely redesign it to stick to clean core principles, or just leave it as is—while weighing the technical challenges and benefits tied to each option.

In the end, this whole process will give you a solid plan for handling your custom code in the implementation project. You’ll have a clear idea of what needs to be fixed, where you can take advantage of new features in SAP S/4HANA, and how to start thinking about cloud solutions that can help streamline your operations and make maintaining custom code easier.

Integration Review and Redesign

 In this stage, it's important to take a good look at the current integrations in your SAP ERP system. The goal is to come up with a solid plan to handle any impacts, whether that means fixing things up or swapping out older integrations for newer SAP technologies. Here are a few key things you should think about:

  • Analyze Current Integrations: Start by using SAP Readiness Check 2.0 and the ABAP test cockpit to check out all your inbound and outbound integrations. Figure out which interfaces are affected, and you might even need to tweak some business processes to make sure they work smoothly with SAP S/4HANA.
  • Define the Target Integration: Next, lay out a game plan for replacing outdated technologies with tools like SAP Integration Suite or SAP Process Orchestration. The idea is to move away from old methods like IDocs to more modern options like APIs or OData services to improve functionality and keep maintenance straightforward. While you're focusing on the integrations that will be impacted by the system conversion, this is also a great chance to create a roadmap for upgrading all your integrations in the long run.
  • Apply Clean Core Principles: When it comes to integration, it's best to stick to standard SAP methods (like OData, SOAP, and events), and make sure you're orchestrating effectively between SAP S/4HANA and any external platforms. Security also needs to be up to date. Prioritize standard integrations and consider custom interfaces only when there’s no other choice. And if you do have to create custom interfaces, ensure they're properly documented and plan to replace them once a standard option is available.

Test Planning

In this stage, you need to plan, design, and get ready for testing the new system. Some important things you’ll need are a solid test strategy, a test plan, and a schedule that everyone agrees on. It's also crucial to make sure that all necessary prerequisites are sorted out before you dive into end-to-end testing.

Here is what is typically included in a system conversion:

Realize Your Requirements

In the Realize phase, you will convert the development and quality systems. You will also implement new requirements based on the design and tweak the impacted custom code as needed. You will set up the necessary integrations. Once everything is converted, the project team will jump into the planned testing.

Here are some key tasks to focus on during this phase:

Let’s review them more thoroughly:

System Conversion of the Development System

You should kick off the setup of the development system at the start of the realization phase. After that, it’s time to dive into all the necessary functional and technical tasks to convert the development system, sticking to the technical approach you've defined, and following the Runbook documented in the sandbox system. The steps are the ones you already know:

  1. Technical pre-conversion activities
  2. Functional pre-conversion activities
  3. Executing the system conversion
  4. Functional post-conversion activities

Additional Activities in the Converted Development System

Once you've wrapped up the technical conversion of the development system, there are a few important tasks you need to tackle to get everything ready for testing. Here’s the lowdown:

  • Enable SAP Fiori: First off, you need to set up the SAP Fiori apps that we've chosen during the fit-to-standard workshops. From there, you can run the system activation to get things rolling. After you lay the groundwork, you’ll need to create custom business roles and set up spaces and pages in the SAP Fiori launchpad so everyone can try out the app navigation. Depending on how those tests go, you might have to tweak the spaces, pages, and roles a bit.
  • Custom Code Remediation: Next up is fixing the custom code. You’ll start with a prioritized list of the affected objects that may need to be cleaned up or redesigned to fit into a “clean core” concept. This step involves making functional tweaks and optimizing performance, along with potentially refreshing the user experience.
  • Fixing Integrations: You’ll need to make necessary updates to the interfaces and replace any outdated integrations highlighted during the Integration Review phase. This means modifying some custom code, incorporating new standard integrations, and creating workarounds for custom scenarios if standard options aren’t available.
  • Solution Configuration: You’ll configure additional features in iterative cycles, focusing on the business scenarios and process requirements you outlined earlier. This will normally occur in two-week sprints, during which you’ll tackle system setup, documentation, and unit testing according to the agreed-upon backlog. Every change will be documented, and you'll collect all customizing transports along the way.
  • Enhancements and Features: Fill in any gaps you’ve identified. SAP suggests leveraging the extensibility capabilities in SAP BTP to tackle these gaps. For, that, you’ll follow a three-tier extensibility model:
    • Tier 1 is for cloud development using the ABAP Cloud coding language.
    • Tier 2 is for custom API wrappers that temporarily use legacy code until you can establish a proper Tier 1 solution.
    • Tier 3 covers legacy development for situations where the ABAP Cloud model won’t cut it but could be transitioned to Tier 1 or 2 later.

  • Testing Remediated Custom Code: Finally, you need to double-check that the custom code is all sorted after the remediation process. The dev team should be hands-on with tools like ABAP test cockpit and ABAP SQL Monitor for code and performance quality checks. It’s also essential to use some advanced coding techniques to spot any additional improvements that automated tools might miss. Just a heads-up: you’ll be running these steps in the customer client to avoid using client 000.

System Conversion Execution of the Quality System

Before you dive into integration testing, you need to convert the quality assurance system. You already know the process. So, make sure to carry out all the necessary functional and technical tasks following the Runbook.

Testing

In the testing phase, you’ll roll out the strategy and plan you came up with during the Explore phase. You’ll also check the testing tools and make sure the testing teams get proper training. Then you break the test plan down into a detailed schedule of activities.

As you run the tests, you formally log any defects for tracking purposes. You fix issues and move those fixes through the system, retesting until everything is sorted out. Since errors can be hidden in different process variations, you make sure the test scripts cover a good mix of those variations. All the different teams come together to make the testing process work smoothly.

Cutover Preparation

When you're getting ready to convert the production system, having a solid cutover plan is key. This plan lays out all the steps you'll need to take from the prep work right up to the final conversion. Usually, there’s someone designated as the cutover manager to keep everything on track.

While the cutover plan doesn’t dive deep into the technical nitty-gritty like a runbook would, it’s good to pull out some key tasks from there to keep the process on track. Plus, it’s wise to include a backup plan in case the conversion needs to be rolled back for any reason.

 

Deploy & Run Your Solution

In this part of the project, you’ll run a final dress rehearsal before the big launch. Based on the result, you can confirm if you are good to go. In summary, you will validate that everything’s set for the final conversion.

The last step of the SAP Activate methodology is the Run phase. Once the system conversion project is successfully wrapped up, companies and project teams dive into optimizing how the new SAP S/4HANA solution works. In this ongoing phase, the operations team makes sure everything runs smoothly.

Here are some key tasks to focus on during these phases:

Let’s check them out more closely.

System Conversion Execution of the Dress Rehearsal System

Before you actually cut over to the production system, you need a final practice. This should be created from the latest version of the SAP ERP production system. Once that's done, you should run through all the functional and technical tasks outlined in the cutover plan. If you find anything that needs tweaking, you can update the runbook or the cutover plan.

The point of this rehearsal is to make sure everyone knows their roles, the order of tasks, and how long everything will take. Since the cutover schedule can get complicated with lots of moving parts and people involved from both IT and the business side, good communication is super important for making it all work smoothly. What you’ll find during this rehearsal will help you make the go/no-go decision.

System Conversion Execution of the Production System (Cutover)

Everything’s set on the organizational, business, functional, technical, and system fronts for your project to go live. To wrap up the production system conversion, you need to tackle all the tasks in the cutover plan that you’ve worked through in the earlier cycles. Just make sure to follow the detailed steps in the runbook.

System Go-Live and Hypercare Support

The system's go-live is the last big step in a successful SAP S/4HANA conversion project. At this point, everything is up and running in the production environment, and then you move into the hypercare phase to help everyone get used to the new system.

Hypercare is the period right after go-live where you're here to tackle any questions or issues that pop up. You’ll be doing daily checks to keep a close eye on how well the new solution is being adopted. It’s super important to see how the system is performing and make any tweaks if needed.

Continuous Improvement and Innovation

You need to set up some IT maintenance processes to continuously improve the solutions. This means regularly rolling out feature packs and support packs to get the latest updates from SAP. It’s important for both the business and IT teams to work together on a planning cycle to decide which new features to implement.

Those improvements include deploying the UX strategy defined during the Prepare Phase and replacing integrations and custom code following the Clean Core approach defined during Explore.

System Upgrade

Upgrading your system means updating your SAP S/4HANA solution to the latest version of the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. It's not just about the technical stuff; it also includes making improvements like adding new features, fixing bugs, and boosting security. Keeping up with these upgrades is super important for staying efficient, making sure everything works well with new tech, and keeping your system secure.

If you want to know more about the upgrade process—including the tools you'll use, the different phases you'll go through, and the activities involved—there’s a detailed roadmap in the SAP Activate Roadmap Viewer. For more details, check out this blog post: Upgrade your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition following SAP Activate.

We've come to the end of this blog post.  Just a reminder, this was a quick overview of the tasks you can find in the SAP Activate Roadmap Viewer for RISE with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition—System Conversion. Check out the details in the Roadmap Viewer to help you run your system conversion project.

I hope you found this blog post helpful. If you did, please give it a "like" or share it. I’d love to hear your thoughts and feedback, so feel free to leave a comment. If you have any questions, just drop them in the Q&A section with the tags SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. We'll try to get back to you as soon as we can.


Thanks,

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA RIG team.

entry.title: Re: Partial transfer of finished goods to another plant f...
entry.content:
Hello Community,

Thank you for sharing this business scenario. Based on your requirements, here’s how you can handle this process in SAP using standard QM (Quality Management) and PP (Production Planning) functionalities:

post the goods receipt for the full quantity (e.g., 10 units) to the manufacturing plant's stock.

Use SAP QM to create an inspection lot for the finished goods. This can be triggered automatically during the goods receipt if the material is subject to inspection.

Define a sampling procedure in QM to determine the quantity to be inspected (e.g., 3 out of 10 units). This can be configured in transaction QP01.

Transfer the sampled quantity (3 units) to the inspection location or another plant for quality inspection. This can be done using a stock transfer order (transaction VL10B) or a movement type (e.g., 321 for stock transfer to quality inspection).

Perform the quality inspection and record the results in SAP using transaction QA11.

After inspection, if the goods pass quality checks, post the inspected quantity back to unrestricted stock at the manufacturing plant or the target plant. The remaining quantity (7 units) is already in unrestricted stock at the manufacturing plant.

- Yes, this process can be automated within standard SAP functionality. Here’s how:

  • Automatic Inspection Lot Creation: Configure the material master (QM view) to automatically create an inspection lot during goods receipt. This is done by setting the Inspection Lot Origin and Inspection Lot Creation indicators in the material master.

  • Automatic Stock Transfer: Use SAP’s output determination and workflow to automate the stock transfer for the sampled quantity. This can be achieved by defining rules in the system to trigger the stock transfer based on the inspection lot creation.

  • Results Recording and Stock Posting: Automate the posting of inspection results and stock updates using SAP’s batch management and quality notifications.

ademt

entry.title: Re: How to Handle BMR (Batch Manufacturing Record) pharma...
entry.content:
Thank you for reaching out to the SAP Community. Managing Batch Manufacturing Records (BMR) in SAP PP without using PI Sheets on Fiori is indeed a challenge, but there are alternative approaches you can consider. Below are some suggestions:

1. Use SAP PP-PI (Process Industry) Functionality
- SAP PP-PI is designed for process industries and provides robust functionality for managing batch manufacturing records. You can leverage master recipes, process orders, and batch management to handle BMRs effectively.
- Master recipes and process orders can capture all the necessary steps, materials, and instructions required for batch production. This can serve as a digital BMR.

2. Custom Fiori Apps
- If your client prefers a Fiori-based solution, you can develop custom Fiori apps to manage BMRs. These apps can integrate with SAP PP to capture and display batch-related data, instructions, and production steps.
- The app can pull data from process orders, master recipes, and batch records, providing a user-friendly interface for operators and supervisors.

3. SAP DMS (Document Management System)
- SAP DMS can be used to manage BMRs as documents. You can create and store BMRs as PDFs or other document formats and link them to process orders or batches.
- This approach allows you to maintain a digital record of BMRs without relying on PI Sheets.

4. SAP MII (Manufacturing Integration and Intelligence)
- SAP MII can be used to create custom interfaces for managing BMRs. It can integrate with SAP PP and other systems to provide real-time data and instructions for batch production.
- MII can also be used to develop web-based interfaces for operators to access BMRs.

5. Enhanced Process Order Cockpit
- You can enhance the standard Process Order Cockpit in SAP PP to include additional fields or tabs for BMR-related information. This can be achieved through custom developments or user exits.

I hope these suggestions help you find the right approach for managing BMRs in SAP PP. If you need further clarification or assistance, feel free to ask!

Best regards,
ademt.

entry.title: Why S/4HANA is the Future of SAP Integration?
entry.content:
Why S/4HANA is the Future of SAP Integration

1. Shift from PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite

SAP PI/PO has been a trusted middleware for years, but SAP is now focusing on SAP Integration Suite as the future of integration. Cloud-based, scalable, and equipped with pre-built integrations, SAP Integration Suite is designed to support modern hybrid and cloud-native architectures.

2. Rise of API-Driven and Event-Based Integration

Traditional middleware relied heavily on message-based integration. With S/4HANA and SAP BTP, the focus has shifted towards API-first and event-driven integration, making SAP CPI (Cloud Platform Integration) and Event Mesh key components for the future.

3. Embedded AI and Process Automation

S/4HANA brings built-in AI, machine learning, and robotic process automation (RPA) capabilities, reducing manual interventions and improving efficiency. Integration consultants must now adapt to these intelligent technologies to stay relevant.

4. Growing Demand for Cloud Integration

Many organizations are adopting hybrid and multi-cloud strategies, requiring seamless integration between on-premise SAP systems and cloud applications (e.g., Salesforce, Workday, AWS). SAP Integration Suite and SAP BTP play a crucial role in enabling these integrations.

5. End of Mainstream Support for Legacy Systems

SAP PI/PO will reach the end of mainstream support in the coming years, pushing enterprises to migrate. SAP has clearly outlined its roadmap for cloud-based integration, making it imperative for consultants to align their skills accordingly.

How to Upgrade Your SAP Integration Skills

  1. Learn SAP Integration Suite – Get hands-on experience with SAP CPI, API Management, and Event Mesh.
  2. Understand S/4HANA Business Processes – Knowing how different modules interact helps in designing efficient integrations.
  3. Master Cloud and Hybrid Integration Patterns – Learn how to integrate SAP with third-party cloud applications and non-SAP systems.
  4. Explore AI and Automation – Familiarize yourself with SAP AI Core, RPA, and intelligent workflows.
  5. Stay Updated with SAP Roadmap – Keep an eye on SAP’s evolving integration strategies and upcoming features.

Final Thoughts

The role of an SAP Integration Consultant is evolving rapidly. The shift towards cloud, API-first strategies, and intelligent automation is redefining how integrations are built and managed. By upgrading your skills to S/4HANA and SAP Integration Suite, you not only future-proof your career but also position yourself as a valuable asset for enterprises navigating their digital transformation journey.

Are you ready to embrace the future of SAP integration? The time to upgrade is now!

entry.title: Asset Accounting - Asset Revaluation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502 Release
entry.content:
Introduction

All our accounting records are based on historical costs. However, with socio-economic development, the past amounts recorded in accounting may not reflect the true fair value, which allows the enterprise to revalue its assets. For example, a commercial real estate property purchased by the company in 2010 had a recorded value of 300,000 CNY. By 2020, the value had increased to 600,000 CNY. Consequently, the recorded value of 300,000 CNY no longer accurately reflects the true and fair value, necessitating a revaluation.

For manual asset revaluation, you can use the "Post Asset Revaluation" application (F7775). Previously, assets could be revalued simultaneously in all or multiple currencies. Typically, revaluations are performed in multiple currencies.

However, in some cases, you may need to perform a revaluation only in the local currency, without affecting the group currency (hard currency). For this purpose, the new business transaction type "Revalue in Selected Currency" is now available. Using this new business transaction type, you can post revaluations in a single (selected) currency and report this change.

This means you can now differentiate between standard revaluation situations (all affected currencies) and specific revaluations (only one currency in all ledgers).

Scenario

An asset was acquired on 12/31/2024 , the depreciation key is CD01. The useful life is 4 years. The depreciation is straight-line. And the scrap value percentage is 5%.

Up to now, the fixed asset has the following values:

APC:

100,000

Planned Depreciation for 2025:

-23,750

Net book value at year-end:

76,250

The formula for asset depreciation in 2025 is 100000*(1-5%)/48*12=23750

After asset acquisition, the planned depreciation has been calculated accordingly.

Due to inflation, a revaluation will be required at the end of the year 2024. The book value of the asset needs to be revalued by 20%.

On the selection screen of the Post Asset Revaluation app, you specify the following values:

For example:

  • A revaluation of the APC of 20,000 CNY and
  • A corresponding revaluation of ordinary depreciation of 5,000 CNY

For manual revaluations, you can use the Post Asset Revaluation app (F7775). Previously, the app allowed you to revaluate in all or several currencies simultaneously. Typically, revaluations are performed in more than one currency.

However, in certain cases, you may need to perform the revaluation in the local currency only, without affecting the group currency (hard currency).

The S4HC 2502 version supports the selection of ‘Revalue in Selected Currency’ in the Business Transaction Type field for a specific revaluation (only one currency across all ledgers).

After revaluation, the APC and depreciation have been adjusted accordingly, and the planned depreciation amount for 2025 is calculated as [(100,000 + 20,000) * (1 - 5%) - 5,000) / 48 * 12] = 27,250.

Except manual revaluation, as of 2502 version, there is a new app Maintain Asset Revaluation Index which introduce the feature about automatic revaluation with revaluation index in a specific depreciation area.

You use this app if you want to periodically perform automatic revaluations based on index values.

Detailed instructions can be found in the link

Here share some results of steps:

  1. Create revaluation index (mandatory)
  2. Assign index ID to asset master record (mandatory)
  3. Check values in the valuation view for the fixed asset (optional)
  4. Check values in asset history sheet (optional)

Step 1: Create revaluation index (mandatory)

Step 2: Assign index ID to asset master record (mandatory)

Step 3: Check values in the valuation view of the manage fixed assets app (optional)

According to revaluation index assigned to the asset, the revaluation amount and depreciation amount will be planned as designed below.

For year 2024

For year 2025

For year 2026

For year 2027

Conclusion

This new features allow for more flexibility in managing asset revaluation, enabling you to handle specific revaluation scenarios more effectively.

entry.title: Re: How to add more than 1 email notification recipient i...
entry.content:
Unfortunately, there is a limitation in SAP S/4HANA that allows only one email recipient to be added for job notifications in transaction SM37. The system does not provide a built-in way to add multiple email recipients for a single job [1].

However, there are some potential workarounds you could consider:

  1. Create a distribution list: Set up a distribution list or group email address that includes all the desired recipients. Use this single email address as the recipient in SM37 [1].

  2. Custom ABAP program: Develop a custom ABAP program to modify the email recipient information for multiple jobs. This approach requires careful implementation and thorough testing [1].

  3. Individual job configuration: If you have a limited number of jobs, you may need to configure each job separately with different email recipients [1].

It's important to note that these limitations apply specifically to the job scheduling functionality in SM37. Other SAP modules or applications may have different capabilities for sending notifications to multiple recipients.

For example, in SAP SuccessFactors, it is possible to add multiple recipients when emailing resumes or CVs to other users within the company [2][3]. However, this functionality is specific to the SAP SuccessFactors module and is not applicable to the core S/4HANA job scheduling system.

If managing multiple email recipients for job notifications is a critical requirement for your organization, you may want to consider exploring third-party job scheduling tools that integrate with SAP S/4HANA and offer more flexible notification options.

entry.title: Re: Diagnostic agent support on older windows version for...
entry.content:
Dear customer,

Windows 7 support ends January 14, 2020, see also SAP Note 2749767.

Windows Server 2012, Windows Server 2012 (R2) and Windows 8 / 8.1 support ends October 10, 2023, see also SAP Note 3281142 and 66971.

As the SAP JVM 8.1 PL >= 97 is required for Diagnostic Agent installation with Software Provisioning Manager 1.0 SP43 (for SWPM 1.0 SP41, JVM 8.1 PL>=92 is required), if an OS version is deprecated, newer SAP JVM 8.1 patches are not guaranteed to work or to be secure and compliant on this OS version any more. 

Please kindly refer to following notes for the statement.

2253383 - Diagnostics Agent - SWPM Archive-Based Installation

1858920 - Diagnostics Agent installation with Software Provisioning Manager

2749767 - Support of SAP Products on Windows Server 2008  (R2) after January 2020

3281142 - Support of SAP Products on Windows Server 2012 (R2) after October 2023

2998013 - SAP JVM 8.1 As Runtime for SAP Tools - Additional Information

Best regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: 销售订单审批流
entry.content:
前言

对于销售订单审批流，一直没有一篇完整的中文Blog，这里写一篇手把手教会的配置过程和介绍最新的APP：灵活工作流管理用来方便监控审批流。

客户和实施方有客制化需求需要沟通的可以在留言区留言，我们可以一起合作完成审批流的搭建。

本博文参考英文Blog:Flexible Workflow for Sales for Public Cloud - Com... - SAP Community

前提配置

1.SSCUI：102751, Define Reasons for Approval Requests (定义审批请求的原因)

2.SSCUI：102572，Assign Reasons for Approval Requests(分配审批请求的原因)

3.APP：管理团队和职责

4.APP：自定义逻辑，参考BADI: SD_APM_SET_APPROVAL_REASON. 

这步骤是最关键的一步，如果审批流没有触发成功，请优先检查这一步，案例使用的是简单场景，如果客户场景比较复杂又不知道如何实现可以留言。

Example Code 1:

" ***** Start

if salesdocument-sddocumentcategory = 'C'  and "Sales Order document category is "C"; Beware Sales Order without charge is "I"

   salesdocument-salesdocumenttype = 'TA'  and "Internal Key values are available in the Badi (TA and not OR)

   salesdocument-salesorganization = '1310'.

   salesdocapprovalreason = 'ZFC1'.

   return.

endif.

" ***** End

5.APP：管理销售凭证工作流

系统实现

销售订单创建人: Chen
一级审批: Fancy
1.创建销售订单，触发审批流

当销售订单处于审批中的状态时，是无法进行更改的

2.审批人收到通知后要求重新返工

3.当销售订单被审批人设置重新返工后，销售订单可以编辑，此时我们可以通过APP：灵活工作流管理来查看审批流的状态，如果审批流触发有问题，我们可以通过此APP重新触发并且查看日志。

4.更改销售订单后重新触发审批流，审批人审批通过销售订单

5.再次通过APP:灵活工作流管理查看审批流状态

注意

如果客户需求当审批中的销售订单不能参与MRP，请参考Blog:

Stop requirement transfer when sales order is in a... - SAP Community

感谢观看

entry.title: Posting Net Pay to Two Different GL Accounts
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

As per our client’s requirement, the /559 net pay balance sheet needs to be posted in two different GL accounts:

Fixed Pay posted to one GL account.
Incentives and Variable Pay posted to a different GL account.
All deductions are based on the fixed salary only. Additionally, the Wages Payable is posted under the fixed component, and the remaining Incentive GL is posted separately.

Could anyone provide insights or suggestions on how to set up this configuration in SAP Payroll to meet this requirement? Any guidance on how to handle the GL postings effectively would be highly appreciated.

Currently /559 posted one GL - Wage payable ( Balance sheet)

Thank you in advance for your help!

Best regards,

Jeevanantham

entry.title: Experience at SAP CodeJam Kolkata 2025 || 4th March & 5th March, 2025
entry.content:
Reflecting on SAP CodeJam 2025 - Kolkata 


I’m thrilled to share my experience attending the SAP CodeJam 2025 in Kolkata, driven by SAP Developer advocates @noravonthenen , @Vitaliy-R , @sheenamk  and @eshrinivasan  hosted by Capgemini this year! The session centered around Machine Learning (ML) with Python and Generative AI (GenAI) in SAP, and it was an eye-opener into how these groundbreaking technologies are shaping the future of enterprise solutions.

Some key insights that stood out:

🤖 Empowering SAP Consultants with Real-Time AI: We discussed how GenAI and ML can help SAP consultants tackle real-time challenges. AI is enhancing efficiency and reducing the time spent on manual interventions. This is a game-changer in real-time project scenarios!

📊 Predictive Analytics for Smarter Decisions: With ML models embedded in SAP for smarter decision-making, such as optimizing processes, forecasting demand, and identifying risks—leading to more proactive, data-driven strategies.

⚡ Real-Time Problem Solving: GenAI tools are revolutionizing issue resolution during SAP implementations. By offering automated, instant troubleshooting, consultants can focus on high-value strategic decisions rather than spending time on repetitive system fixes.

🔧 Tailored Solutions at Speed: The ability of GenAI to generate custom code, configurations, and solutions in real-time allows SAP consultants to swiftly address client needs, significantly reducing implementation times while delivering highly personalized, impactful outcomes.

Here are some use cases and benefits of GenAI and ML with Python in SAP:

Use Cases:

  1. Predictive Analytics:

    • Use Case: Use ML models to forecast demand, optimize inventory, and predict financial trends in SAP.
    • Benefit: Helps businesses make data-driven decisions, reduce costs, and improve forecasting accuracy.

  2. Process Automation (RPA):

    • Use Case: Automate repetitive tasks like data entry, report generation, or invoice processing using Python scripts and GenAI.
    • Benefit: Reduces manual labor, minimizes errors, and accelerates process completion.

  3. Customer Insights and Personalization:

    • Use Case: Leverage ML to analyze customer behavior data and create personalized product recommendations in SAP.
    • Benefit: Improves customer satisfaction and drives sales by delivering more tailored experiences.

  4. Anomaly Detection:

    • Use Case: Detect outliers or unusual transactions in financial or inventory data using ML models trained in Python.
    • Benefit: Identifies potential fraud or errors quickly, enhancing security and data integrity.

  5. Automated Reporting and Dashboards:

    • Use Case: Use GenAI to generate custom reports and dashboards based on business parameters in SAP.
    • Benefit: Provides real-time insights without manual report creation, improving decision-making speed.

  6. Supply Chain Optimization:

    • Use Case: Use ML algorithms to optimize supply chain processes in SAP, from procurement to delivery.
    • Benefit: Reduces operational costs, minimizes delays, and enhances inventory management.

Benefits:

  1. Enhanced Efficiency:

    • Automates repetitive tasks, reducing manual effort and increasing overall system efficiency.

  2. Improved Accuracy:

    • ML models enhance decision-making accuracy by providing data-driven insights and predictions.

  3. Cost Savings:

    • Reduces costs by improving forecasting, streamlining processes, and identifying inefficiencies.

  4. Scalability:

    • Python and AI-powered models can scale to meet the growing data needs of businesses, ensuring systems can handle increased complexity over time.

  5. Faster Decision-Making:

    • Real-time insights from GenAI and ML lead to quicker and more informed decisions.

By integrating Python, GenAI, and ML into SAP, businesses can unlock automation, predictive capabilities, and smarter analytics, driving significant business value.

Apart from these, many key-points are taken away i.e. types of ML ( Supervised, Unsupervised, Reinforcement Learning ), Training a data model, Business Data Cloud, Data Preparation, Feature Engineering, Data Frame, EDA etc. from @Vitaliy-R on 4th March, Getting Started with Machine Learning using SAP HANA. Again on 5th March, Getting Started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core session by @noravonthenen key-points ( AI Buzzwords )are taken away i.e. Fine Tuning, Orchestration including Grounding, Prompt Templating, Content Filter, Data Masking, LLM Access and all the hands-on procedure i.e. define Orchestration service deployment, AI model deployment etc.

Kudos to Chatrath Harinder Singh, @Sharmistha22 , Goutam Dasgupta, Abhishek Guha Roy for helping to make this event successful.

Also thank you @DataTherapist for believing in me to make this event a success. Also, thank you @Raya_16 and @TanushreeD  to make this event in Capgemini.

@ShilpaSAP @lauraschmitz @thomasjungblu @Abhishek_10 @KamallicaGhosh1 @Soham_SCM @SohamBhKoL 

Also sharing some of the glimpses of the SAP CodeJam Kolkata 2025.


#SAP #AI #MachineLearning #GenAI #FutureOfSAP #Capgemini #SAPCodeJam2025 #SAPRoadshow

entry.title: Reflections on SAP Code Jam Kolkata 2025 || 4th March & 5th March, 2025
entry.content:
Experience on SAP Code Jam 2025 - Kolkata

Thrilled to have attended SAP Code Jam 2025 in Kolkata, hosted by Capgemini this year. Big thanks to incredible SAP Developer Advocates @noravonthenen, @Vitaliy-R , @sheenamk and @eshrinivasan for driving such an insightful session.  The focus on Machine Learning (ML) with Python and Generative AI (GenAI) in SAP was truly an eye-opener. It’s exciting to see how these cutting-edge technologies are transforming the future of enterprise solutions!

Real-Time Scenario and Benefits of GenAI & ML with Python in SAP:

Scenario: Predictive Maintenance in Manufacturing

 

  • Situation: A manufacturing company uses SAP to manage its production line. Machines are integrated into SAP with real-time data being captured.

 

  • GenAI & ML Use: Machine Learning algorithms (implemented using Python) analyze historical data from equipment sensors (e.g., temperature, vibrations) to predict when a machine is likely to fail.

 

  • Outcome: Using GenAI models, SAP provides real-time alerts and recommendations for maintenance before a breakdown occurs, reducing downtime.

Benefits:

  1. Cost Savings: Reduces costly unplanned downtime and expensive repairs by predicting maintenance needs in advance.
  2. Efficiency Boost: Minimizes machine failure disruptions, ensuring smoother operations and higher productivity.

Data-Driven Decisions: SAP, powered by ML, uses real-time data to enhance decision-making, optimizing maintenance schedules and resource allocation.

Scenario: Sales Forecasting and Demand Planning

  • Situation: A retail company uses SAP for managing inventory and sales. They need to predict demand for products across different regions and seasons.
  • GenAI & ML Use: ML models built with Python analyze historical sales data to predict future product demand, factoring in trends, weather, holidays, and promotions.

Outcome: GenAI helps generate highly accurate sales forecasts, adjusting inventory levels proactively.

Benefits:

  1. Improved Forecast Accuracy: Generates more accurate sales forecasts, minimizing overstock or stockouts.
  2. Operational Efficiency: Optimizes inventory management, reducing storage costs and improving order fulfillment.
  3. Proactive Decision-Making: Allows business leaders to adjust strategies based on accurate, real-time demand predictions.

    In real-time, GenAI and ML with Python empower SAP systems to automate decision-making, forecast trends, and optimize business processes, driving efficiency and profitability.

     

    Kudos to Chatrath Harinder Singh,  @Sharmistha22 , Goutam Dasgupta, Abhishek Guha Roy for helping to make this event successful.

    Also thank you      for @DataTherapist believing in me to make this event a success. Also, thank you @Sayontan_Das  and @TanushreeD   to make this event in Capgemini.

     @ShilpaSAP @lauraschmitz @thomasjungblu @Abhishek_10 @KamallicaGhosh1 @Soham_SCM @SohamBhKoL 

    Also sharing some of the glimpses of the SAP CodeJam Kolkata 2025.

 

#SAP #AI #MachineLearning #GenAI #FutureOfSAP #Capgemini #SAPCodeJam2025 #SAPRoadshow

entry.title: Re: How to set Default Printer
entry.content:
Hi ramco1917,

As DI API does not appear to have the Printer Setting functionality, there is a workaround that will require some manual work to set the desired printer in the layout (rpt) file itself.

These are the steps to apply the workaround:

  1. Export the layout from Report and Layout Manager saving it as an rpt file.
  2. Open the rpt file in Crystal Reports, change the printer in the File → Page Setup dialog and save the file.
  3. Import the rpt file in Report and Layout Manager.
  4. Repeat the steps above to each layout that will be sent via DI API to a non-default printer.

Kindly help to test the above mentioned steps and share whether it helps for your cause or not.

Kind regards,

ANKIT CHAUHAN

SAP Business One Support

SYSTEM-CALL OBJMGR CLONE lo_original TO lo_clone.

entry.title: Re: SAP Analytics Cloud Planning : Advance Formulas (Aggr...
entry.content:
Hi @SANA11 

Please refer to the Advanced formulas script and validate the calculated result.

 

MEMBERSET [d/Entity] = "REG0001"

VARIABLEMEMBER #All_Product OF [d/PRODUCT]

//Aggregate "Units" & "Lookup_Price" of Product and write it on VARIABLEMEMBER #All_Product
DATA([d/PRODUCT] = #All_Product) = RESULTLOOKUP()

//Calculate Revenue to multiply Aggregated "Units" & "Lookup_Price" of Product and write it on Product "#" and Account "Revenue"
DATA([d/PRODUCT] = "#", [d/Account] = "Revenue") = RESULTLOOKUP([d/PRODUCT] = #All_Product, [d/Account] = "Units") *
                                                   RESULTLOOKUP([d/PRODUCT] = #All_Product, [d/Account] = "Lookup_Price")

 

I don't have an idea about the Account dimension Member ID, so please replace it with your actual Account dimension Member ID, which is Account dimension member description is listed in my script.

I hope this is helpful to you, and if I have misunderstood anything, please feel free to reach out.

moonjun

entry.title: Re: Maintenance plans with status "Scheduled Save to call "
entry.content:
Hi Malhadri,

We don't have direct reports which display plans with 'Save-to-call' status. But a development can be made using the below given logic to get notified when the plan's call reach 'Save-to-call' status;

-> Pass the WARPL (Maintenance Plan number) in MHIS Table. If the respective line item's HORDA (Call Date) = Current Date (SYDATE), then send notification to the maintenance team for saving the scheduling.

Hope this was helpful.

Regards,
Ritesh PR

entry.title: Re: Purchase Requisition Item Fiori App Enhancement witho...
entry.content:
Hello,

Yes, it is feasible. You just extend the data model for Preq Item context using Custom Fields and Logic app, then your new fields are part of the data model for PREQ, so they are available for extensions, BAdIs, etc.. Afterwards you create a new Adaptation Project in BAS for the app, add the field to the Valuation section, thus creating Object Page controller extension, where you can implement any UI logic if needed, but preferably for any calculation logic it is batter to implement this on the backend, via BAdI for Pur. Req. (look for *MM*PUR* End. Spot, don't remember the name right now) - there is a separate method for processing the item.

I delivered a lot of Manage PREQ app extensions like this.

Regards,
Rado

entry.title: Re: CRM Copy Badi Service contract to Order
entry.content: Hi Niranjan, Apologogies there was a typo. You would rather use CRM_COPY_BADI. Once implemented, don’t forget to activate the implementation via transaction SE19, and ensure the copy control is set up correctly in customizing (SPRO → CRM → Transactions → Basic Settings → Define Copying Control for Business Transactions). You should consider the BAdI approach once source and target document types differ. Another approach would be the following one: https://community.sap.com/t5/crm-and-cx-q-a/crm-copy-control-crm-copy-badi-for-document-relationship/qaq-p/3774685
